sections.label: Section 348
sections.original: γίγνονται πάνθʼ ὅ τι βούλονται · κᾆτʼ ἢν μὲν ἴδωσι κομήτην
sections.translation: They become all things , whatever they please . And then if they see a person with long hair , a wild one of these hairy fellows , like the son of Xenophantes , in derision of his folly , they liken themselves to centaurs .

sections.label: Section 351
sections.original: τί γὰρ ἢν ἅρπαγα τῶν δημοσίων κατίδωσι Σίμωνα , τί δρῶσιν ;
sections.translation: Why , what , if they should see Simon , a plunderer of the public property , what do they do ?

sections.label: Section 352
sections.original: ἀποφαίνουσαι τὴν φύσιν αὐτοῦ λύκοι ἐξαίφνης ἐγένοντο .
sections.translation: They suddenly become wolves , showing up his disposition .

sections.label: Section 353
sections.original: ταῦτʼ ἄρα ταῦτα Κλεώνυμον αὗται τὸν ῥίψασπιν χθὲς ἰδοῦσαι ,
sections.translation: For this reason , then , for this reason , when they yesterday saw Cleonymus the recreant , on this account they became stags , because they saw this most cowardly fellow .

sections.label: Section 355
sections.original: καὶ νῦν γʼ ὅτι Κλεισθένη εἶδον , ὁρᾷς , διὰ τοῦτʼ ἐγένοντο γυναῖκες .
sections.translation: And now too , because they saw Clisthenes , you observe , on this account they became women .

sections.label: Section 356
sections.original: χαίρετε τοίνυν ὦ δέσποιναι · καὶ νῦν , εἴπερ τινὶ κἄλλῳ ,
sections.translation: Hail therefore , O mistresses ! And now , if ever ye did to any other , to me also utter a voice reaching to heaven , O all-powerful queens .

sections.label: Section 358
sections.original: χαῖρʼ ὦ πρεσβῦτα παλαιογενὲς θηρατὰ λόγων φιλομούσων ,
sections.translation: Hail , O ancient veteran , hunter after learned speeches ! And thou , O priest of most subtle trifles ! Tell us what you require ?

sections.label: Section 360
sections.original: οὐ γὰρ ἂν ἄλλῳ γʼ ὑπακούσαιμεν τῶν νῦν μετεωροσοφιστῶν
sections.translation: For we would not hearken to any other of the recent meteorological sophists , except to Prodicus ; to him , on account of his wisdom and intelligence ; and to you , because you walk proudly in the streets , and cast your eyes askance , and endure many hardships with bare feet , and in reliance upon us lookest supercilious .

sections.label: Section 364
sections.original: ὦ γῆ τοῦ φθέγματος , ὡς ἱερὸν καὶ σεμνὸν καὶ τερατῶδες .
sections.translation: O Earth , what a voice ! How holy and dignified and wondrous !

sections.label: Section 365
sections.original: αὗται γάρ τοι μόναι εἰσὶ θεαί , τἄλλα δὲ πάντʼ ἐστὶ φλύαρος .
sections.translation: For , in fact , these alone are goddesses ; and all the rest is nonsense .

sections.label: Section 366
sections.original: ὁ Ζεὺς δʼ ἡμῖν , φέρε πρὸς τῆς γῆς , οὑλύμπιος οὐ θεός ἐστιν ;
sections.translation: But come , by the Earth , is not Jupiter , the Olympian , a god ?

sections.label: Section 367
sections.original: ποῖος Ζεύς ; οὐ μὴ ληρήσεις · οὐδʼ ἔστι Ζεύς .
sections.translation: What Jupiter ? Do not trifle . There is no Jupiter .

sections.label: Section 367b
sections.original: τί λέγεις σύ ;
sections.translation: What do you say ? Who rains then ? For first of all explain this to me .

sections.label: Section 369
sections.original: αὗται δήπου · μεγάλοις δέ σʼ ἐγὼ σημείοις αὐτὸ διδάξω .
sections.translation: These to be sure . I will teach you it by powerful evidence . Come , where have you ever seen him raining at any time without Clouds ? And yet he ought to rain in fine weather , and these be absent .

sections.label: Section 372
sections.original: νὴ τὸν Ἀπόλλω τοῦτό γέ τοι δὴ τῷ νῦν λόγῳ εὖ προσέφυσας ·
sections.translation: By Apollo , of a truth you have rightly confirmed this by your present argument . And yet , before this , I really thought that Jupiter caused the rain . But tell me who is it that thunders . This makes me tremble .

sections.label: Section 375
sections.original: αὗται βροντῶσι κυλινδόμεναι .
sections.translation: These , as they roll , thunder .

sections.label: Section 375b
sections.original: τῷ τρόπῳ ὦ πάντα σὺ τολμῶν ;
sections.translation: In what way ? you all-daring man !

sections.label: Section 376
sections.original: ὅταν ἐμπλησθῶσʼ ὕδατος πολλοῦ κἀναγκασθῶσι φέρεσθαι ,
sections.translation: When they are full of much water , and are compelled to be borne along , being necessarily precipitated when full of rain , then they fall heavily upon each other and burst and clap .

sections.label: Section 379
sections.original: ὁ δʼ ἀναγκάζων ἐστὶ τίς αὐτάς , οὐχ ὁ Ζεύς , ὥστε φέρεσθαι ;
sections.translation: Who is it that compels them to borne along ? Is it not Jupiter ?

sections.label: Section 380
sections.original: ἥκιστʼ ἀλλʼ αἰθέριος Δῖνος .
sections.translation: By no means , but aethereal Vortex .

sections.label: Section 380b
sections.original: Δῖνος ; τουτί μʼ ἐλελήθειν ,
sections.translation: Vortex ? It had escaped my notice that Jupiter did not exist , and that Vortex now reigned in his stead . But you have taught me nothing as yet concerning the clap and the thunder .

sections.label: Section 383
sections.original: οὐκ ἤκουσάς μου τὰς Νεφέλας ὕδατος μεστὰς ὅτι φημὶ
sections.translation: Have you not heard me , that I said that the Clouds , when full of moisture , dash against each other and clap by reason of their density ?

sections.label: Section 385
sections.original: φέρε τουτὶ τῷ χρὴ πιστεύειν ;
sections.translation: Come , how am I to believe this ?

sections.label: Section 385b
sections.original: ἀπὸ σαυτοῦ ʼγώ σε διδάξω .
sections.translation: I ’ ll teach you from your own case . Were you ever , after being stuffed with broth at the Panathenaic festival , then disturbed in your belly , and did a tumult suddenly rumble through it ?

sections.label: Section 388
sections.original: νὴ τὸν Ἀπόλλω καὶ δεινὰ ποιεῖ γʼ εὐθύς μοι , καὶ τετάρακται
sections.translation: Yes , by Apollo ! And immediately the little broth plays the mischief with me , and is disturbed and rumbles like thunder , and grumbles dreadfully :

sections.label: Section 390
sections.original: ἀτρέμας πρῶτον παππὰξ παππάξ , κἄπειτʼ ἐπάγει παπαπαππάξ ,
sections.translation: at first gently pappax , pappax ; and then it adds papa-pappax ; and finally , it thunders downright papapappax , as they do .

sections.label: Section 392
sections.original: σκέψαι τοίνυν ἀπὸ γαστριδίου τυννουτουὶ οἷα πέπορδας ·
sections.translation: Consider , therefore , how you have trumpeted from a little belly so small ; and how is it not probable that this air , being boundless , should thunder so loudly ?

sections.label: Section 394
sections.original: ταῦτʼ ἄρα καὶ τὠνόματʼ ἀλλήλοιν βροντὴ καὶ πορδὴ ὁμοίω .
sections.translation: For this reason , therefore , the two names also Trump and Thunder , are similar to each other . But teach me this , whence comes the thunderbolt blazing with fire , and burns us to ashes when it smites us , and singes those who survive . For indeed Jupiter evidently hurls this at the perjured .

sections.label: Section 398
sections.original: καὶ πῶς ὦ μῶρε σὺ καὶ Κρονίων ὄζων καὶ βεκκεσέληνε ,
sections.translation: Why , how then , you foolish person , and savouring of the dark ages and antediluvian , if his manner is to smite the perjured , does he not blast Simon ,

sections.label: Section 400
sections.original: οὐδὲ Κλεώνυμον οὐδὲ Θέωρον ; καίτοι σφόδρα γʼ εἴσʼ ἐπίορκοι ·
sections.translation: and Cleonymus , and Theorus ? And yet they are very perjured . But he smites his own temple , and Sunium the promontory of Athens , and the tall oaks . Wherefore , for indeed an oak does not commit perjury .

sections.label: Section 403
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδʼ · ἀτὰρ εὖ σὺ λέγειν φαίνει . τί γάρ ἐστιν δῆθʼ ὁ κεραυνός ;
sections.translation: I do not know ; but you seem to speak well . For what , pray , is the thunderbolt ?

sections.label: Section 404
sections.original: ὅταν ἐς ταύτας ἄνεμος ξηρὸς μετεωρισθεὶς κατακλῃσθῇ ,
sections.translation: When a dry wind , having been raised aloft , is inclosed in these Clouds , it inflates them within , like a bladder ; and then , of necessity , having burst them , it rushes out with vehemence by reason of its density , setting fire to itself through its rushing and impetuosity .

sections.label: Section 408
sections.original: νὴ Δίʼ ἐγὼ γοῦν ἀτεχνῶς ἔπαθον τουτί ποτε Διασίοισιν
sections.translation: By Jupiter , of a truth I once experienced this exactly at the Diasian festival ! I was roasting a haggis for my kinsfolk , and through neglect I did not cut it open ; but it became inflated and then suddenly bursting , befouled my eyes and burned my face .

sections.label: Section 412
sections.original: ὦ τῆς μεγάλης ἐπιθυμήσας σοφίας ἄνθρωπε παρʼ ἡμῶν ,
sections.translation: O mortal , who hast desired great wisdom from us ! How happy will you become among the Athenians and among the Greeks , if you be possessed of a good memory , and be a deep thinker , and endurance of labour be implanted

sections.label: Section 415
sections.original: ἐν τῇ ψυχῇ , καὶ μὴ κάμνεις μήθʼ ἑστὼς μήτε βαδίζων ,
sections.translation: in your soul , and you be not wearied either by standing or walking , nor be exceedingly vexed at shivering with cold , nor long to break your fast , and you refrain from wine , and gymnastics , and the other follies , and consider this the highest excellence , as is proper a clever man should , to conquer by action and counsel , and by battling with your tongue .

sections.label: Section 420
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὕνεκά γε ψυχῆς στερρᾶς δυσκολοκοίτου τε μερίμνης
sections.translation: As far as regards a sturdy spirit , and care that makes one ’ s bed uneasy , and a frugal spirit and hard-living and savory-eating belly , be of good courage and don ’ t trouble yourself ; I would offer myself to hammer on , for that matter .

sections.label: Section 423
sections.original: ἄλλο τι δῆτʼ οὖν νομιεῖς ἤδη θεὸν οὐδένα πλὴν ἅπερ ἡμεῖς ,
sections.translation: Will you not , pray , now believe in no god , except what we believe in--this Chaos , and the Clouds , and the Tongue--these three ?

sections.label: Section 425
sections.original: οὐδʼ ἂν διαλεχθείην γʼ ἀτεχνῶς τοῖς ἄλλοις οὐδʼ ἂν ἀπαντῶν ·
sections.translation: Absolutely I would not even converse with the others , not even if I met them ; nor would I sacrifice to them , nor make libations , nor offer frankincense .

sections.label: Section 427
sections.original: λέγε νυν ἡμῖν ὅ τι σοι δρῶμεν θαρρῶν , ὡς οὐκ ἀτυχήσεις
sections.translation: Tell us then boldly , what we must do for you ? For you shall not fail in getting it , if you honour and admire us , and seek to become clever .

sections.label: Section 429
sections.original: ὦ δέσποιναι δέομαι τοίνυν ὑμῶν τουτὶ πάνυ μικρόν ,
sections.translation: O mistresses , I request of you then this very small favour , that I be the best of the Greeks in speaking by a hundred stadia .

sections.label: Section 431
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἔσται σοι τοῦτο παρʼ ἡμῶν · ὥστε τὸ λοιπόν γʼ ἀπὸ τουδὶ
sections.translation: Well , you shall have this from us , so that hence-forward from this time no one shall get more opinions passed in the public assemblies than you .

sections.label: Section 433
sections.original: μὴ ʼμοί γε λέγειν γνώμας μεγάλας · οὐ γὰρ τούτων ἐπιθυμῶ ,
sections.translation: Grant me not to deliver important opinions ; for I do not desire these , but only to pervert the right for my own advantage , and to evade my creditors .

sections.label: Section 435
sections.original: τεύξει τοίνυν ὧν ἱμείρεις · οὐ γὰρ μεγάλων ἐπιθυμεῖς .
sections.translation: Then you shall obtain what you desire ; for you do not covet great things . But commit yourself without fear to our ministers .

sections.label: Section 437
sections.original: δράσω ταῦθʼ ὑμῖν πιστεύσας · ἡ γὰρ ἀνάγκη με πιέζει
sections.translation: I will do so in reliance upon you , for necessity oppresses me , on account of the blood-horses , and the marriage that ruined me . Now , therefore , let them use me as they please .

sections.label: Section 440
sections.original: τουτὶ τοὐμὸν σῶμʼ αὐτοῖσιν
sections.translation: I give up this body to them to be beaten , to be hungered , to be troubled with thirst , to be squalid , to shiver with cold , to flay into a leathern bottle ,

sections.label: Section 445
sections.original: θρασὺς εὔγλωττος τολμηρὸς ἴτης
sections.translation: if I shall escape clear from my debts , and appear to men to be bold , glib of tongue , audacious , impudent , shameless , a fabricator of falsehoods , inventive of words , a practiced knave in lawsuits , a law-tablet , a thorough rattle , a fox , a sharper , a slippery knave , a dissembler , a slippery fellow , an impostor ,

sections.label: Section 450
sections.original: κέντρων μιαρὸς στρόφις ἀργαλέος
sections.translation: a gallows-bird , a blackguard , a twister , a troublesome fellow , a licker-up of hashes . If they call me this , when they meet me , let them do to me absolutely what they please . And if they like ,

sections.label: Section 455
sections.original: νὴ τὴν Δήμητρʼ ἔκ μου χορδὴν
sections.translation: by Ceres , let them serve up a sausage out of me to the deep thinkers .

sections.label: Section 457
sections.original: λῆμα μὲν πάρεστι τῷδέ γʼ
sections.translation: This man has a spirit not void of courage , but prompt . Know , that if you learn these matters from me , you will possess among mortals a glory as high as heaven .

sections.label: Section 461
sections.original: τί πείσομαι ;
sections.translation: What shall I experience ?

sections.label: Section 462
sections.original: τὸν πάντα χρόνον μετʼ ἐμοῦ
sections.translation: You shall pass with me the most enviable of mortal lives the whole time .

sections.label: Section 465
sections.original: ἆρά γε τοῦτʼ ἄρʼ ἐγώ ποτʼ
sections.translation: Shall I then ever see this ?

sections.label: Section 467b
sections.original: ὥστε γέ σου πολλοὺς ἐπὶ ταῖσι θύραις ἀεὶ καθῆσθαι ,
sections.translation: Yea , so that many be always seated at your gates , wishing to communicate with you and come to a conference with you , to consult with you as to actions and affidavits of many talents , as is worthy of your abilities .

sections.label: Section 476
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἐγχείρει τὸν πρεσβύτην ὅ τι περ μέλλεις προδιδάσκειν ,
sections.translation: But attempt to teach the old man by degrees whatever you purpose , and scrutinize his intellect , and make trial of his mind .

sections.label: Section 478
sections.original: ἄγε δὴ κάτειπέ μοι σὺ τὸν σαυτοῦ τρόπον ,
sections.translation: Come now , tell me your own turn of mind ; in order that , when I know of what sort it is , I may now , after this , apply to you new engines .

sections.label: Section 481
sections.original: τί δέ ; τειχομαχεῖν μοι διανοεῖ πρὸς τῶν θεῶν ;
sections.translation: What ? By the gods , do you purpose to besiege me ?

sections.label: Section 482
sections.original: οὔκ , ἀλλὰ βραχέα σου πυθέσθαι βούλομαι .
sections.translation: No ; I wish to briefly learn from you if you are possessed of a good memory .

sections.label: Section 483b
sections.original: δύο τρόπω νὴ τὸν Δία ·
sections.translation: In two ways , by Jove ! If anything be owing to me , I have a very good memory ; but if I owe unhappy man , I am very forgetful .

sections.label: Section 486
sections.original: ἔνεστι δῆτα μανθάνειν ἐν τῇ φύσει ;
sections.translation: Is the power of speaking , pray , implanted in your nature ?

sections.label: Section 487
sections.original: λέγειν μὲν οὐκ ἔνεστʼ , ἀποστερεῖν δʼ ἔνι .
sections.translation: Speaking is not in me , but cheating is .
